howItWorks.intro: This calculator sizes tankless water heaters by determining the total GPM demand from simultaneous fixtures and the temperature rise needed to heat groundwater to your desired temperature.
rules: Flow rate (GPM) = sum of all simultaneously running fixture flow rates
Temperature rise = desired hot water temp − incoming groundwater temp
BTU required = flow rate (GPM) × temperature rise × 8.33 (weight of water) × 60 min ÷ efficiency
Gas units are rated by BTU output (typically 150,000–199,000 BTU for whole-house)
Electric units are rated by kW (typically 18–36 kW for whole-house)
howItWorks.outro: Gas tankless heaters require proper venting (direct vent or power vent) and adequate gas line sizing (typically 3/4" minimum). Electric units need dedicated circuits (usually 2-3 circuits at 40-60 amps each). Always check local codes and have a licensed plumber install.

faq.question: What size tankless water heater do I need for a family of 4?
faq.answer: A family of 4 typically needs 7-10 GPM with a gas unit rated at 150,000-199,000 BTU. This handles 2 showers and a faucet simultaneously. In cold climates (40°F groundwater), you may need the higher end. Electric units need 24-36 kW for equivalent performance.

faq.question: Gas vs electric tankless: which is better?
faq.answer: Gas tankless heaters deliver higher flow rates (8-11 GPM) and handle more simultaneous fixtures. Electric units are simpler to install, have no venting requirements, and are more efficient (99% vs 80-97%), but deliver lower flow rates (3-8 GPM) and require significant electrical upgrades.

faq.question: What is temperature rise and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Temperature rise is the difference between incoming cold water and your desired hot water temp. In Minnesota (40°F groundwater, 120°F desired), you need an 80°F rise. In Florida (70°F groundwater), only a 50°F rise. Higher rise means lower flow rate from the same unit.

faq.question: Can a tankless water heater replace a 50-gallon tank?
faq.answer: Yes, but sizing matters. A 50-gallon tank delivers about 60-80 gallons in the first hour. A properly sized tankless unit provides unlimited hot water but at a fixed flow rate. A 199,000 BTU gas unit can match or exceed a 50-gallon tank for most households.

faq.question: How much does a tankless water heater save?
faq.answer: Tankless heaters save 8-34% on energy costs depending on hot water usage. Homes using less than 41 gallons/day save 24-34%. Homes using 86+ gallons/day save 8-14%. Annual savings are typically $75-$150. The units last 20+ years vs 10-15 for tank heaters.

faq.question: Do I need to upgrade my gas line for tankless?
faq.answer: Usually yes. Tank heaters use 40,000-50,000 BTU on a 1/2" gas line. Tankless units need 150,000-199,000 BTU, requiring a 3/4" line minimum (often 1" for long runs). A plumber must verify gas line capacity, meter size, and regulator before installation.
